items.stem: Market interest rates fall sharply. What happens to the price of an existing fixed-coupon bond, and why?

options.text: It rises — its fixed coupon is now more attractive than what new bonds offer
options.explanation: Correct. The old bond's above-market coupon is suddenly scarce. Buyers bid its price up until its yield falls into line with the new, lower market rate.

options.text: It falls — lower rates mean the issuer is weaker
options.explanation: That mixes the channels. Rates are the price of time, not a statement about this issuer's health. A pure rate fall lifts existing bond prices.

options.text: Nothing — the contract fixed all the payments
options.explanation: The payments are fixed; the price is not. The market reprices the fixed stream continuously — that gap is the core of this whole path.

options.text: It rises only if the issuer raises the coupon
options.explanation: Issuers don't adjust coupons on outstanding fixed-rate bonds. The price does all the adjusting.

items.stem: In 2022, central banks raised rates rapidly — and highly rated bond funds posted large losses. What's the mechanism?

options.text: The funds' bonds defaulted en masse
options.explanation: There was no default wave in high-grade bonds — the issuers kept paying. The losses came through prices, not broken promises.

options.text: Existing fixed-coupon bonds were repriced downward so their yields matched the new, higher market rates
options.explanation: Exactly. Rising rates push every outstanding fixed-rate bond's price down — the see-saw applied to whole portfolios at once. High credit quality means low default risk, not immunity to rate moves.

options.text: Investors panicked irrationally and sold below fair value
options.explanation: No panic required — the repricing is arithmetic. Discount fixed cash flows at higher rates and the fair value itself is lower.

options.text: The funds' managers picked unusually bad bonds
options.explanation: The losses were market-wide, not selection error. Any portfolio of fixed-coupon bonds loses price value when rates jump — the question is how much, which is what the next lessons measure.
